coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of carbon quantum dot/silver nanocomposite and its antimicrobial, catalytic and sensing applications
coredata.dc:creator: Kaur, Jasneet
Bhattu, Monika
Rawat, Mohit
Varma, Rajender S.
Acevedo, Roberto
Shaban, Mohamed
Al-Saeedi, Sameerah I.
Singh, Jagpreet
coredata.dc:description: Environmentally benign synthesis of nanocomposite, a pivotal facet of nanotechnology, and gaining prominence due to the increasing demand for facile, sustainable, and safe synthesis methods. The present research reports an facile/cost-effective method for the preparation of carbon dots (CDs) and carbon dot silver nanocomposites ( CD@AgNCs) via a hydrothermal treatment of peanut shells. The well-dispersed and spherical CDs with an average diameter of 5–6 nm were obtained and further employed for the preparation of CD@AgNCs. The formation of CD@AgNCs. was confirmed by optical and microscopic studies and ared shift in the λ max from 277 nm (CDs) to 450 nm (CD@AgNCs) with a size range of 30-40 nm was observed. The synthesized CD@AgNCs exhibit excellent catalytic potency for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol, and also displaying a unique interaction and sensing ability towards heavy metal ions (Hg2+), causing a pronounced change in color from reddish-brown to transparent with limit of detection (LOD) of 23.47 ppm. Also, the prepared composite exhibit efficient antimicrobial potential against gram-negative (Escherichia coli) bacteria. Consequently, this study delves into a unified effective remediation platform with the integration of catalysis, sensing, and antimicrobial potentials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
Carbon dots/silver nanocomposite
Catalysis
Heavy metals
4-Nitrophenol reduction
Antimicrobial activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots (CDs)
FTIR and DLS analysis of CD@AgNCs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2023R58), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.The authors gratefully acknowledge University of Naples, Italy, Chandigarh University Mohali, Punjab, (India) for research providing necessary resources to carry out this work. The authors express their gratitude to Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2023R58), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Visible absorption spectrum for the C-dots and the inset showing their green fluorescence, (b) FTIR spectrum of the C-dots, (c) HR-TEM images of C-dots presenting, and (d) histogram of nanoparticle diameter distribution.
(a) XRD spectrum and (b) XPS spectrum of the C-dots.
(a) Absorbance spectra and (b) band gap calculation of CDs and CDs-Ag NC derived.
(a) FTIR spectrum of CDs and CD@AgNCs and (b) DLS spectrum of CD@AgNCs.
(a–c) HR-TEM images of CDs-Ag NC at various magnifications, and (d) histogram of nanoparticle diameter distribution.
(a) Plate showing diameters of zones of inhibition of E. coli. (gram negative bacteria); and (b) linear increment of the zone of inhibition with respect to composite volume.

UV–visible spectra illustrating C-dots/Ag nanocomposite intervened catalytic reduction of 4-NP to 4-AP with varying CDs-Ag nanocomposite volumes: (a) 5 μL (15 min), (b) 10 μL (12 min) and (c) 15 μL (9 min); and (d) shows the pseudo-first-order kinetic model study.
Reduction% of the 4-NP using CD@AgNCs of different concentrations (5, 10 and 15 μL) as a function of the time.
UV–Visible absorption spectra show (a) metal sensing profiles of different metal ions and (b) different Hg2+ concentrations.
(a) Interference studies of CD@AgNCs–Hg2+ in presence of other heavy metal ions; and (b) Linearity response of CD@AgNCs towards Hg2+ over a concentration range of 10–100 ppm.
Catalytic efficiency of CD@AgNCs towards 4-NP using different concentrations of catalyst.
Comparative Analysis of reported silver-based nanocomposite for the detection of mercury ion.

row.entry: Gold/iron doped silver iodide supported on polyglycine
Ag-coordination aramid nanofiber-silver nanoparticle composite beads
AgNPs embedded sulfur-doped gCN quantum dots

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of carbon quantum dot/silver nanocomposite and its antimicrobial, catalytic and sensing applications

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Environmentally benign synthesis of nanocomposite, a pivotal facet of nanotechnology, and gaining prominence due to the increasing demand for facile, sustainable, and safe synthesis methods. The present research reports an facile/cost-effective method for the preparation of carbon dots (CDs) and carbon dot silver nanocomposites ( CD@AgNCs) via a hydrothermal treatment of peanut shells. The well-dispersed and spherical CDs with an average diameter of 5–6 nm were obtained and further employed for the preparation of CD@AgNCs. The formation of CD@AgNCs. was confirmed by optical and microscopic studies and ared shift in the λ max from 277 nm (CDs) to 450 nm (CD@AgNCs) with a size range of 30-40 nm was observed. The synthesized CD@AgNCs exhibit excellent catalytic potency for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol, and also displaying a unique interaction and sensing ability towards heavy metal ions (Hg2+), causing a pronounced change in color from reddish-brown to transparent with limit of detection (LOD) of 23.47 ppm. Also, the prepared composite exhibit efficient antimicrobial potential against gram-negative (Escherichia coli) bacteria. Consequently, this study delves into a unified effective remediation platform with the integration of catalysis, sensing, and antimicrobial potentials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, there have been observed a serious rise in environmental pollution as a result of population increase and quick industrialization. The increase of civilization has resulted in environmental damage such as air, soil, and water which pose serious risks to both human health and ecological security. Furthermore, due to the numerous health risks associated with heavy metal ions and other harmful organic compounds found in soil and water, the issue of heavy metal ions is becoming more and more prominent (Jaishankar et al., 2014). Due to the effects of these organic compounds (Bhattu and Singh, 2023) and heavy metal ions, it is necessary to develop a sensitive and reliable sensor system in order to accurately detect, monitor, and quantify these substances (Buledi et al., 2021; Maghsoudi et al., 2021; Melnikov et al., 2022; Wani, 2021; Hussain et al., 2022)). For instance, drinking water contamination contributes to about 14,000 daily deaths caused by water pollution in underdeveloped nations (Rodríguez-Lado et al., 2013). There are currently significant issues due to several new contaminants that cannot be eliminated by conventional water treatment methods (Wang et al., 2013). The myriad of methods have been introduced in order to tackle these rising issues. Despite the fact that these approaches are sensitive and reliable, they are costly to implement, and they require highly qualified and professional personnel to execute them. Due to their cumbersome pretreatment procedures, they are also unsuitable for onsite analysis of analytes.

Among the reported method, nanotechnology-based methods have been proven a boon for all these issues owing to their unique characteristics like optical, electrochemical, photochemical characteristics (Farooq et al., 2022) and a vast number of applications (Hussain et al., 2021) including the biological potential and to address the problems related to water contamination, air contamination, soil contamination and oil spilling (Hussain et al., 2022). For instance, Ni9S8@C was synthesized via one-step reaction and was utilized for methanol oxidation reaction (Hussain et al., 2019) and NiMoO4 nanoparticles were employed for the supercapacitor applications (Hussain et al., 2020). Furthermore, it makes sense to assume that this field will keep growing as more and more serious environmental issues grow on a global scale.

Over twenty years ago, the term “plasmonics” was first used to describe cutting-edge technology that allows for active light manipulation using metallic nanoparticles (Brongersma and Shalaev, 2010). Plasmonics, have been proved a new multidisciplinary scientific and technology subject, and are currently regarded as a panacea due to the wide range of environmental remediation applications including environmental engineering (Wang et al., 2018).However, it has become increasingly critical in recent years to investigate how plasmonic nanoparticles can be designed and functionalized (Atta et al., 2022; Shaban, 2016; Shaban et al., 2019). There are distinct physical and chemical properties of plasma nanoparticles with diameters below 100 nm, which are attributed to their high surface-to-volume ratios, surface plasmon resonance modes, electrical properties, and antiseptic properties (Callegari et al., 2003; Shaban, 2015). A number of plasmonic nanoparticle applications have been proposed, including catalysis, sensors, biological labeling, hydrogen production, water treatment (Lam et al., 2020), nanophotonics, optoelectronics, antimicrobial coatings and drug delivery (Khorsandi et al., 2021), and food processing (Elsayed et al., 2022; F. Mohamed et al., 2021; Motelica et al., 2021; Rosarin et al., 2013; Shams et al., 2019; Shankar et al., 2017; Tajik et al., 2021). This study selected silver (Ag) among several plasmonic nanoparticles owing to its antimicrobial (Ashour et al., 2015) and catalytic capabilities (Nadagouda et al., 2011). As a result of their peculiar optical characteristics, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have also been examined in regards to sensing applications (Gemeel Abd et al., 2018; H. S. H. Mohamed et al., 2021; Rani et al., 2020; Kordy et al., 2022, found that localized surface plasmon resonance (SPR) occur when conduction band (CB) electrons oscillate near the surface of AgNPs at the same time as the incoming light's electric field. There are varied methods available for producing silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), including radiation, Langmuir-Blodgett, chemical, photochemical, electrochemical, and biological processes (Baruwati et al., 2009; Hebbalalu et al., 2013; Moulton et al., 2010; Nadagouda and Varma, 2008). The AgNPsexhibit excellent non-enzymatic catalytic activity for oxygen reduction, as well as offering several distinct advantages over traditional superoxide dismutase immobilization techniques (Liu et al., 2017), including easy synthesis, sensitivity, selectivity, chemical stability, and thermal stability. Despite the benefits of large linear ranges, low detection limits, vast selectivity, and high stability in the detection of O2 •−, the opto-chemical approaches using AgNPs still have some significant limitations due to the large-scale synthesis of AgNPs with high homogeneity in size and shape, the ability for their in-situ functionalization during the preparation with other active quantum dots to enhance the surface area, increase the active sites, and enhance the light confinement, and minimizing the use or production of hazardous substances (Liu et al., 2018; Wildgoose et al., 2006). Apart from these NPs, a significant amount of attention has been drawn to carbon quantum dots (CDs) due to their high permeability, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and sustained optical and fluorescent signals (Jouyandeh et al., 2021; Rabiee et al., 2022; K. Sharma et al., 2021; Sharma et al., 2018; Singh et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2016). As a result of their hydrophilic functional groups and surface-reducing properties, carbon-based dots can serve both as a reductant as well as a stabilizer when developing AgNPs in situ, resulting in the formation of simple CD and Ag nanoparticle hybrids (Gul et al., 2020; Iravani and Varma, 2020; Ma et al., 2017; Shaik et al., 2021). After treating with an oxidant, the fluorescence of CDs was reduced by medium-sized AgNPs (5 nm). To enhance the fluorescence emission, this study employed a hydrothermal blend approach to create core-shell silver and carbon dots nanocomposite (CD@AgNCs) (Liang et al., 2020). Motivating from the applications of nanocomposites, this study introduces an easy and expedient method for preparing carbon quantum dot/silver nanocomposites at room temperature (CD@AgNCs). A unique aspect of this study is the preparation of CDs from peanut shells through hydrothermal methods, which were used for the first time as both reducing agents and stabilizing agents in the assembly of CD@AgNCs. CD@AgNCs are examined using different techniques in order to determine their optical, morphological, and structural properties. The catalytic reduction of the 4-nitrophenol to the 4-aminophenol is achieved by employing the synthesized CD@AgNCs. An explosive outcome of the interaction between nanocomposites and heavy metal ions is the development of a novel colorimetric detection method. This is based on the interaction between nanocomposites and heavy metal ions. Furthermore, the nanocomposite was examined for its antimicrobial properties against E. coli.

The chemical namely 4-nitrophenol, silver nitrate, sodium borohydride and metal nitrate solutions like copper nitrate, chromium nitrate, mercuric nitrate, magnesium nitrate, zinc nitrate, nickel nitrate, cobalt nitrate, cadmium nitrate, and ferrous nitrate were procured from Sigma Aldrich. Deionized water was utilized for the solution and sample preparation.

The CDs are synthesized by the most efficient synthetic hydrothermal route and the ensued CDs through this method display magnificent luminescent properties. The precursor which is the aqueous extract of outer shell of the peanut is sealed in the autoclave and then the autoclave is placed in the hot air oven at a temperature of 190 °C for a time span of 8 h at a temperature rise rate of 55 °C/min. After that, the product is grounded and mixed with DI water. The mixture is placed in an ultrasonic bath to attain a well-dispersed solution and then centrifuged. The supernatant contains the CDs which exhibit green luminescence under UV illuminator.

A solution of 10–2 M silver nitrate (AgNO3) is prepared. Equal amounts of the CDs and AgNO3 solution are utilized. The CDs solution is poured into the beaker and mild stirred. In this experiment, a solution of silver nitrate (AgNO3) is gradually added to CDs solution in order to attach silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) to the surface of the CDs. This process is called surface functionalization, and it involves modification of the outer layers of the CDs with AgNPs. Basically, the addition of AgNO3 to the CDs solution causes a reduction reaction to occur, which leads to the formation of AgNPs on the surface of the CDs due to the chemical interaction between the Ag + ions in the solution and the functional groups present on the CDs. The color of the solution changed from colorless to reddish-brown, approving the complete reduction of AgNO3 (Ag + to Ag0) and the formation of the nanocomposite (CD@AgNCs).

A Shimadzu/UV-2600 spectrophotometer was used to obtain the absorption spectra of synthesized CDs and CD@AgNCs composites. Phytochemicals were detected in an alpha Fourier-transform infrared spectrophotometer in order to determine how they interacted with CDs and Ag in the extract. Using a Malvern-ZEN-1690 instrument, the average particle size (Z-average) of the subsequent AgNPs was measured. In order to determine the precise particle size and surface morphology, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR TEM; Jeol JEM-2100) was performed.

To explore the antimicrobial activity of CD@AgNCs composite, the strain of the gram-negative bacterium E. coli was introduced in the experiment. Variable amounts of the CD@AgNCs composite were added to cultured E. coli plates and their anti-bacterial properties were examined. The wells in the agar plates were labelled as 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 and filled with CD@AgNCs nanocomposite with varying amounts 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 μL of CD@AgNCs. Distinct zones of inhibition were discerned in all the wells filled with CD@AgNCs nanocomposites, indicating the effective anti-microbial activity of the composite.

The progress for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) was examined by UV–Vis. absorption analysis wherein sodium borohydride (NaBH4) solution (0.37 mL of 0.2 M) was treated with a solution comprising 4-NP (0.037 mL of 0.005 M) in deionized water (1.65 mL); 4-NP was transformed to a 4-nitrophenolate anion. Further, a varying amount of CD@AgNCs as catalysts were added and the reaction was examined spectrophotometrically at various time intermissions; In the reaction, slow alteration of the solution color occurred from bright yellow to colorless.

The detection abilities of the synthesized CD@AgNCs were investigated for the heavy metal ions. To assess the change in the spectral properties of assorted metal ions upon aqueous diffusion of CD@AgNCs, the UV–vis spectrum of CD@AgNCs dispersion (2 mL) was evaluated in presence of 5 μM aqueous solution of the numerous metal ions, such as Hg2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Co2+ Cr2+, Cu2+ and Fe2+. Hg2+ exhibited the maximum variation (enhancement ) in the absorption spectra of the CD@AgNCs which suggests the selectivity of CD@AgNCs towards Hg2+ among all the screened metal ions. Hence, Hg2+ was further undertaken into consideration in order to find the selectivity and sensitivity of the sensor and the corresponding spectra were recorded; UV spectra were monitored and obtained after 2 min of interaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots (CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method as given in section 2.2. Further, the formation of CDs was confirmed using U.V. Visible absorption spectra as illustrated in Fig. 1 (a). This spectrum shows an absorption peak centered at 277 nm which is accredited to n-n* transition of the C =C/C =N bond. The synthesized CDs displayed green fluorescence when exposed to UV as presented in the inset of Fig. 1(a) due to the presence of surface defects in the CDs.

The presence of functionalities in the CDs was examined by the FTIR spectrum shown in Fig. 1(b). The observed bands at 3819, 3732, 3544, 3096, 2830, 2344, 1700, 1378, 1033 and 695 cm−1 in the spectrum represent the –OH, –NH, –CH, =C–H, –CC-, CO and an epoxy ring, respectively (Ma et al., 2017). The presence of these functional groups was attributed towards the water-soluble property of the synthesized CDs.

The nanomorphology of CDs was characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) analysis, Fig. 1(c). As depicted in these images, the well dispersed ensued CDs exhibited a nano-spherical morphology. Fig. 1(d) shows a histogram for the CDs diameter distribution. The mean diameter of CDs was observed to be 6.0 ± 1.6 nm. However, some particles of much large size have stemmed from limited agglomerations and the mean diameter of these particles was found to be 14.7 ± 4.0 nm.

Moreover, the structural parameters of CDs were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) Fig. 2 (a,b). XRD is being used to examine the phase crystallinity of the synthesized CDs as presented in Fig. 2(a). There is a broad peak at 22.36° representing the highly disordered carbon atoms confirming the amorphous nature of synthesized CDs. To recognize the chemical details of the bond compositions and investigate the surface states of the synthesized CDs, XPS spectrum was acquired as presented in Fig. 2(b). The full XPS spectrum displays three characteristic peaks at 275, 425, and 570 eV for C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. This indicates that the CDs comprised C, N, and O elements. In the high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum, the C1s band can be deconvoluted into three binding energy peaks at 275.6, 276.1, and 280.3 eV which are ascribable to CC, C–O, and CO, respectively. The N1s spectrum displays two peaks at 425.0 eV, attributed to two –NH groups. O1s spectrum exhibits two primary bands at 570.9 eV and 571.6 eV, affirming the existence of C–OH/C–O–C and CO, respectively which are in full agreement with FTIR analysis.

The synthesis of composite was carried as per given in experimental section. The formation of CD@AgNCs was preliminary analyzed using UV–visible absorbance spectra. Fig. 3 (a) depicts the absorption spectra of CDs and CD@AgNCs. The absorption spectrum of the CDs displays an absorbance band (λmax) at 277 nm, whereas the spectrum of CD@AgNCs shows an absorbance band at 450 nm. i.e., the composite shows the red shift of λmax. The red shift in the peak confirms the successful attachment of the AgNPs to the CDs. The SPR of CD@AgNCs was observed at 450 nm, substantiating the reduction of pristine Ag ions (Ag+) and the subsequent capping of the AgNPs with CDs (Loiseau et al., 2019). The symmetrical shape of the SPR band and its high intensity refers to the larger population of monodispersed particles and the limitation of the aggregations (Loiseau et al., 2019).

Using the absorbed absorption data, initially, the absorption coefficient (α) can be computed as per equation (1)(Shaban et al., 2017)
(1)
α = 2.303
A ρ
L C
where ρ represents nano powder density, L measured in the unit of quartz thickness (1.0 cm), and C represents nano powder concentration.

Further, Tauc's equation was used to calculate the direct optical bandgap of nanopowders by plotting the Tauc's plot (Ahmed et al., 2020)
(2)
〖
(
α h υ
)
〗 ˆ 2 = G
(
h υ − E _ g
)
where, α is the absorption coefficient, h is the Planck constant, ν is the frequency of the photon, G is the constant and Eg is the band gap of the electron between the conduction and valence band.The intersection of the tangent line with the horizontal axis in Tauc's plot Fig. 3(b) denotes the bandgap transition as an intercept point for a curve i.e., Eg = hν when α = 0.

The obtained Eg values are 4.0 and 5.0 eV for CDs, and 2.2 and 4.4 eV for CD@AgNCs, which confirm the blue shift of the band gap of the composite relative to that of the CDs. This blue shift is related to the particle size effect, whereas the blue shift occurred with increase in the particle size. In the split-off band, deep states may be incorporated to cause the blue shift by introducing resonance structures (states). In Fig. 3(b), the nanocomposite contains two band gaps, one in the UV range and one in the visible range, hence it is ideal for use in sensing and catalytic reduction processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR and DLS analysis of CD@AgNCs
ce:section.ce:para: To better understand the chemical composition and function of CDs and CD@AgNCs nanocomposites, FTIR analysis was employed. CDs have a broad absorption band due to the presence of C–OH and –NH2 surface functionalities. The band at 1700 cm−1 is believed to be due to C–C hydrogen bond surrounding the aromatic ring. The characteristic FTIR absorption bands at 1362 and 1390 cm−1 are attributed to the phenolic –OH functionalities, and the band at 1048 cm−1 represents the C–O groups (Ma et al., 2017). In addition, aromatic C–H out-of-plane bending vibrations are associated with bands at 609, 664, and 705 cm−1.

Similar functionalities, including stabilization of the nanocomposites, are observed for CD@AgNCs nanocomposite structures. The absorption band at 1535 cm−1 in CD@AgNCs nanocomposite is attributed to the conjugated C–O functional group, or quinone. As a consequence of the reduction process, structural changes are evident in CDs.

The outcomes clearly ascertained that these green synthesized CDs encompass surfaces retain several amino and phenol hydroxyl functionalities, together serving as electron donors for reducing Ag ions (Ag+) to the elemental silver (Ag0).

DLS analysis (Fig. 4 (b)) is employed to calculate the particle size distribution and hydrodynamic diameter of the CDs and CD@AgNCs as DLS represents the average particle size of the CD@AgNCswhich was found to be 120 nm with a polydispersity index (PDI) value of 0.325. These data explained the enhancement of the surface by loading CDs on the surface of AgNPs, which were further analyzed using HRTEM analysis.

The morphological analysis of CD@AgNCs was performed using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images at various magnifications (Fig. 5 (a–c)). These analysis showed the homogeneous spherical nanoparticles. Fig. 5(c) reveals the homogenous distribution of the CDs on the exterior of AgNPs which are self-assembled on the surface of the AgNPs, thus offering nano porous features and the diameters of the observed pores being less than 3 nm. These nano-porous features enhance the surface area and increase the hotspot density, which is immensely important for sensing and catalytic applications. Fig. 5(d) displays a histogram for the nanoparticle diameter distribution, which indicates the average particle diameter is 77 ± 18 nm.

CD@AgNCs nanocomposites were evaluated for their antimicrobial properties against the E. coli bacterium. Fig. 6 (a) depicts E. coli (gram-negative bacteria) inhibition by CD@AgNCs solutions at various concentrations. Fig. 6(b) depicts a study of E. coli inhibition zones. The diameter of the zone of clearance increased linearly with an increase in the volume of the composite suspension added to the culture dishes, in accordance with a comparative study of composite volume as a function of antibacterial potential. The CD@AgNCs has demonstrated significant antimicrobial efficacy against Escherichia coli, as revealed by the results of the antimicrobial screening assessment, indicating the potential for CD@AgNCs to be utilized as an effective antimicrobial agent.

Further, the CD@AgNCs were explored for their catalytic potential towards the reduction of 4-nitrophenol. For this purpose, sodium borohydride (NaBH4) was employed as an reducing agent. The reaction solution disappeared in color when CD@AgNCs were introduced as a catalyst and started reducing 4-NP instantly. Fig. 7 shows the spectrophotometrically measured kinetics of the reaction. As 4-NP formed nitrophenolate ions in the presence of NaOH, a sharp band of UV–visible light appeared at 400 nm. While, a rapid decrease in the absorption intensity at 400 nm was observed when CD@AgNCs were added to the reaction medium and a new wider band at 298 nm started emerging indicating the formation of 4-amino phenol (4-AP). Fig. 7(a–c) shows UV–visible spectra for 4-NP a reduction and 4-AP formation using CD@AgNCs nanocomposites.

A pseudo-first-order kinetic model was used to estimate the reduction rate constant. As shown in Fig. 7(d), a linear relationship between the normalized concentration (ln Ct/C0) and reaction time was derived using different volumes of CD@AgNCs solution in order to compare the catalytic activity of CD@AgNCs. In plot ln (Ct/C0) versus time, C0 is the initial concentration of 4-nitrophenolate at time t, while Ct is the final concentration of 4-nitrophenolate at time t. Based on the slopes of the logarithmic plots, the rate constants for the 5, 10, and 15 μL catalyst volumes were observed to be 0.0612, 0.0773, and 0.1423 min-1, respectively which indicates the increase in the rate of reaction (reduction) with increasing concentration of catalyst.

Further, the reduction percentage of 4-NP was plotted against time using different volumes of catalyst solution (5, 10, and 15 μL of CD@AgNCs) which also indicates the increase in the reaction rate with increasing reaction time and catalyst volume (Fig. 8, Table 1
).

Excellent catalytic capabilities are displayed by AgNPs and CDs and thus by CD@AgNCs towards the transformation of 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol. The deprotonation of 4-nitrophenol into phenolate ion is made easier by the presence of a reducing agent (CD@AgNCs). By adsorbing both substances to its surface, the catalyst (CD@AgNCs) in the solution further reduces 4-nitrophenol into 4-aminophenol. Excess NaBH4 causes the pH of the medium to rise and so prevents the BH4− ion from degrading (Scheme 1 ) (Singh et al., 2018)

Further, the sensing potential of prepared CD@AgNCs was evaluated against various kinds of metal ions. As the CDs in CD@AgNCs encompass a carbon skeleton, edge, and surface modified with many oxygen and nitrogen comprising groups. It is expected that –NH2 groups bind to the nanoparticle surfaces and –OH and –COO- groups would lead to the stabilization of CD@AgNCs in water by employing repulsive electrostatic forces. After the addition of Hg2+ solution to the ensued CD@AgNCs solution, the color of the solution alters from reddish-brown to light brown and its color diminishes slowly after the addition of Hg2+and ultimately becomes colorless once a certain amount of Hg2+ was added, whereas the addition of other divalent cation including Cd2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Cr2+, Li2+, Fe2+, and Mg2+ does notaffect the absorbance spectra of CD@AgNCs.Fig. 9 illustrates the UV–Visible absorption spectra for (a) sensing profiles of different metal ions and (b) different Hg2+ concentrations.

Further in order to check the selectivity of CD@AgNCs, interference studies were carried out in which the absorption spectra of CD@AgNCs–Hg2+ was observed in presence of all other competing ions. For this purpose, 100 ppm solution of the other metal cations was added to the solution of CD@AgNCs–Hg2+, interestingly, no significant change has been observed in the absorbance of CD@AgNCs–Hg2+ which suggests the high selectivity of prepared CD@AgNCs towards Hg2+in presence of other heavy metal ions (Fig. 10 (a)).

Moreover, to evaluate the sensitivity of the sensor, limit of detection and limit of quantification has been calculated using 3 sigma method (Bhattu et al., 2022). For this, a linear plot has been plotted by taking the absorbance maxima value into consideration as y-axis and concentration of Hg2+ as x-axis (Fig. 10(b)). The LOD and LOQ were found to be 23.47 ppm and 71.11 ppm, respectively and the reported sensor is obseved to be highly efficient towards the detection of Hg2+ ion (Table 2 ).

The observed phenomenon is possibly associated with the redox reaction between zero-valent silver and Hg2+. It is anticipated that in subsequent Hg2+ addition to the CD@AgNCs, Hg2+ got bound to the Ag surface to displace the biological stabilizer entities from the surface and hence the redox reaction would occur. Under basic conditions, CDs are negatively charged, indicating in situ reduction of Ag + to Ag0. CDs naturally coordinate with the functional groups. A carboxylic, carbonyl, or phenolic group is needed for metal nanoparticles for getting attached to carbon. As AgNPs are generated on CD surfaces, CDs located near the AgNPs prevent them from aggregating in water.

A facile solution-phase synthesis method is used to prepare CD@AgNC composites by directly reducing AgNO3 using CDs derived from peanut shells. The synthesized composites showed prodigious potentail in environmental remediation applications. It was observed that the CD@AgNCs exhibit a strong catalytic potential towards the 100% reduction of 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol within 9 min. Furthermore, the prepared CD@AgNC composite also showed a strong recognition potential towards Hg2+ and gives a colorimetric variation in color from reddish brown to colourless. The composite showed a linear response over a concentration range of 10–100 ppm. Thus, the synthesized composite demonstrates remarkable efficiency in both biological potential and water remediation, paving the way for diverse multidisciplinary composite synthesis

Jasneet Kaur: Writing - original draft & editing, Investigation; Data curation; Monika Bhattu: Writing - original draft & editing, Investigation; Data curation; Mohit Rawat: Formal analysis, writing review & editing; Rajender S. Varmai: Formal analysis, writing review & editing; Roberto Acevedo: Formal analysis, writing review & editing; Sameerah I. Al-Saeedi: Formal analysis, writing review & editing; Mohamed Shabans: Formal analysis, writing review & editing and Jagpreet Singh: Investigation; Conceptualization, Methodology; Resources; Software; Supervision; Validation; Visualization; Writing - review & editing.
Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2023R58), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge University of Naples, Italy, Chandigarh University Mohali, Punjab, (India) for research providing necessary resources to carry out this work. The authors express their gratitude to Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2023R58), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
